data.title: 2 Glorious Dividends To Retire In Style, Yields +7.5%
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

Many of us are careful with our money, diligently tracking expenses and optimizing our spending, even when there's no financial pressure to do so. Yet, despite our best efforts, maintaining the same lifestyle year after year becomes increasingly expensive. This is the harsh reality of inflation economics - a silent, immoral tax that penalizes the diligent and the cautious more than anyone else.

For those still in the workforce, annual salary raises often serve as a buffer against inflation, helping to preserve purchasing power and lifestyle. Similarly, the interest earned on savings accounts might barely keep pace with inflation, but let's be honest: no one ever got rich by keeping their money in a savings account.

So, how do retirees protect their lifestyle from the erosive effects of inflation? There are many possible approaches that retirees could pursue, but my approach is to let the financial markets pay me a growing amount of money every year to combat the effects of inflation. The global economy is a massive, dynamic machine, with trillions of dollars exchanging hands daily, goods and services are being bought and sold in enormous volumes. Dividends are a way to tap into that enormous amount of capital and direct a portion into your account. 2024 was a record year for dividend payouts, and the global aggregate dividends are projected to stay at $2.3 trillion in 2025, with North America and Asia Pacific delivering strong growth.

By investing in companies that pay substantial dividends and consistently raise them and by strategically reinvesting a portion of your dividend income, you can protect your lifestyle in retirement. Let us now discuss two picks to put this strategy into action.

Pick #1: BTI - Yield 7.7%

Thanks to social stigma and the fear of regulatory blockades on their key products, big tobacco companies have been severely undervalued in recent years. There is a tremendous benefit to buying undervalued companies that pay large dividends; you get paid handsomely to wait for the sentiment to change, and it will be beneficial even if that change is small.

British American Tobacco (BTI), which will be referred to as BAT in this article, is the largest tobacco company, with operations in over 180 markets globally. BAT has significantly outperformed the S&P 500 and the Consumer Staples index, demonstrating the power of resilient cash flows from an indispensable industry.

Why do I say indispensable about a product that is known to be harmful to human health?

  • Widespread use: In 2021, an estimated 46 million U.S. adults reported currently using any tobacco product, including cigarettes, e-cigarettes, cigars, smokeless tobacco, and pipes (including hookah). Among those who used tobacco products, 77.5% reported using combustible products (cigarettes, cigars, or pipes), and 18.1% reported using two or more tobacco products. Smoking has an allure of being cool, popularized by Hollywood. Despite the negative perception towards combustible cigarettes, e-cigarettes and vapes have taken over as the new symbol of modern smoking culture. Marketed as "less harmful" alternatives, they come with sleek designs, customizable flavors, and a tech-driven appeal. They are particularly popular among younger demographics, partly due to social media, influencer culture, and targeted marketing.

  • Deeply ingrained into society (and the political system): Smoking is extremely prevalent in society, and proposals to ban it have political ramifications. Although flavored cigarettes have been banned in the U.S. since 2009, menthol is the lone exception. Menthol cigarettes comprise a third of the industry's total U.S. market share. The Biden administration expressed an interest in banning menthol cigarettes in 2021 and put together a formal proposal, only to postpone the decision fearing a negative reaction from voters repeatedly. The Trump administration has withdrawn this plan altogether. Former U.K. Prime Minister Rishi Sunak proposed an age-tiered smoking ban, which would gradually phase out cigarette sales by preventing younger generations from ever legally purchasing them. However, this bill failed to get the support of his own caucus.

  • Tax Revenues: Tobacco is the most highly taxed consumer product in the United States. In 2023, the U.S. brought in over $37 billion in revenue from taxing cigarettes and other tobacco products at the federal, state, and local government levels. Despite higher taxation (seen as a punitive measure) and consistent price increases, demand for tobacco products remains strong, highlighting its deeply ingrained role in society and the challenges of reducing consumption.

We are not here to debate the morality of investing in tobacco, as moral arguments rarely justify an investment decision in publicly traded companies. Social media's addictive impact on mental health, labor exploitation in countries with weak labor laws, and the dumping of toxic waste into oceans are just a few ethical dilemmas that investors must navigate. Smoking is yet another issue, and ultimately, investors must decide what aligns with their values - and what lets them sleep at night.

Last year, BAT sold a 3.6% stake in India-based ITC Ltd. for £1.57 billion, using the proceeds to repurchase shares. The company continues to maintain a 25% stake in the Indian conglomerate, which went through a demerger last year. As a result, BAT became a 15.3% shareholder in ITC hotels (whose shares were issued to ITC shareholders as part of the transaction). This 15.3% stake is worth $630 million, and BAT's CEO mentioned that the company doesn't see this to be a strategic fit in their portfolio and plans to divest by 2026.

"We have no interest in becoming a long-term shareholder of a hotel chain in India," said Tadeu Marroco, Chief Executive Officer of BAT.

During FY 2024, BAT added 3.6 million adult consumers to a total of 29.1 million to its smokeless products, which represent 18% of FY 2024 revenues. BAT's Vuse maintains an impressive 64.8% market share in Europe, with the percentage exceeding 85% in key markets like France, Poland, Denmark, and Switzerland. Within the European Union, the smokeless business contributes over 30% of the revenues in 18 markets.

BAT is a highly cash-flow-rich business, generating £7.9 billion in FCF during FY 2024, and the company maintains a strong track record of sharing profits with shareholders. BAT maintains a £900 million share buyback plan for 2025 (after £700 million was used in FY 2024). BAT has returned over £30 billion to shareholders since 2018.

With its recent 2% annual dividend raise for FY 2024, BAT boasts 18 years of growing dividend payments, and its current GBp 60.06/share quarterly payment calculates to a 7.7% annualized yield.

Note: BAT is a U.K. corporation that declares and pays dividends in GBP. U.S. investors will receive an amount that is variable based on exchange rates. The U.K. does not withhold taxes on dividends paid to international investors.

From a valuation standpoint, BTI offers a high yield with the highest coverage in its peer group while trading at the lowest multiple. At current prices, we also like Altria (MO), but we will discuss this Dividend King in a separate article.

BAT has warned of headwinds to its combustibles business from increased taxation of tobacco among other products in Bangladesh due to the IMF's special ordinance, and increased regulatory pressures in Australia. Big Tobacco's current valuations are reflective of these headwinds, and BAT is currently leading in the adoption of smokeless products. The company targets becoming a fully smokeless company by 2035, and the company has a strong platform of products that support this vision and are seeing growing adoption in major markets. By 2030, the company aims to have 50 million consumers of its smokeless products.

BAT has had a terrific year, and we expect great things from this company in 2025 and beyond. In an overvalued market, there is a significant benefit to buying discounted companies. When the biggest growth story runs out of steam, reliable cash-flow giants like BAT keep delivering, reminding investors that fundamentals always matter.

Pick #2: CSWC - Yield 10.1%

Capital Southwest Corporation (CSWC) is an internally managed BDC (Business Development Company) that invests in first lien senior secured loans to lower middle market companies with annual EBITDA between $3M and $25M. CSWC is giving us a little extra income this quarter, with regular dividends of $0.58/share and a supplemental dividend of $0.06/share. The supplement dividend last quarter was $0.05, and the BDC has paid a supplemental dividend for 10 quarters in a row.

CSWC has a great track record of producing excess earnings and paying supplements and special dividends. However, investors must note that the supplement can disappear without any fundamental issue with the company. Source

During the third quarter (CSWC's fiscal year ends on March 31st), the BDC reported pre-tax NII of $0.64/share, and NAV remained unchanged at $16.59/share. 95% of CSWC's portfolio was rated 1 or 2, indicating that the borrower is performing at or better than at the time of loan origination.

CSWC pays a 10.1% annualized yield when factoring in the annualized base yield and only the declared supplemental dividends.

Conclusion

Inflation is an unavoidable reality, silently eroding the purchasing power of your savings and threatening your lifestyle and financial security in retirement.

By investing in companies that distribute a portion of their profits to shareholders-and strategically reinvesting a portion of those dividends-you can create a self-sustaining income stream that grows to combat inflation. This approach not only safeguards your purchasing power but also positions you to benefit from the dynamic global markets.

At High Dividend Opportunities, we make this happen through our Rule of 42, which emphasizes deep diversification for reliable income, and the rule of 25, which insists on reinvesting at least 25% of the total income generated back into the portfolio for organic growth, inflation defense, and to take advantage of market volatility. The key to a secure retirement isn't just preserving your wealth-it's ensuring that your income keeps growing, year after year. This is the power of the Income Method, and the beauty of income investing.

data.title: Michael Lee On The AI Revolution - Palantir Still The Clear Leader
data.description:
Originally published on February 20, 2025

By Mike Larson

In this episode of the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast, Michael Lee, founder of Michael Lee Strategies, shares why he believes Palantir Technologies is the clear AI leader and why Wall Street is still underestimating the AI revolution.

Transcript

Larson: At our MoneyShow Symposium in December 2024, my next guest dedicated his entire presentation to one tech stock. No, really, in front of the audience in Sarasota, he called it the clear leader ready to take over the future. And between that date and today, the stock is already up 54%.

What does the 20-plus-year veteran of the financial services industry think about the name now? And what else does he like in the tech sector or otherwise? Let's find out. My guest is Michael Lee, founder of the investment and wealth planning firm, Michael Lee Strategies. Michael, welcome to the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast.

So, Mike, it's not very often that a guest talks about one stock entirely from the main stage, but that's what you did in Sarasota back in December. So what is that stock and why do you do it?

Lee: So that stock is Palantir (PLTR). The stock is up on unconscionable amounts over the last year. I think from the low-20s to almost 120, as we're speaking right now. And it's – it was expensive on a price-to-earnings ratio and a price-to-sales ratio at 20, it's much more expensive now. And the question is why? The world has changed from a data management standpoint. In that, the way data is ingested to a corporation and used on a day-to-day basis is forever changed. We're never going back.

And I'd liken this change to moving from a horse and buggy to an internal combustion engine, or from hand-making things to an all completely automated assembly line. Might even be a better comparison. And that, of course, is artificial intelligence. And so we're, I think, two-and-a-half weeks off of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK), which rocked the markets. And that touched on a lot of topics and the market, read it the opposite way it should have, in that something called Jevons paradox in that where the faster and more advanced a technology gets and the cheaper it gets, the more widely used it is.

And so if I can get 10x or 20x to compute or computing power out of Nvidia (NVDA) GPU now, as before DeepSeek, it makes much more sense to invest in one because my return on investment is exponentially higher. So we're at a turning point, I think, Wall Street is completely missing it.

But to bring it back to Palantir, what's going on with these data is these data – it all goes into a large language model. And for that large language model to be applied to a business, you need a software solution, and Palantir is without competition.

And so as people are starting to get their arms around about how big this artificial intelligence, this data revolution that's going on right now is, they're starting to see that there's a clear leader in the space, and they're $4 billion in revenue next year, may actually be $5 billion and '28, '29, maybe $20 billion or $25 billion. That's $10-plus billion in free cash flow, $15 billion in free cash flow, which could make it $1 trillion company, given the margins and how spectacular their technology is.

And it's - they've been doing this for so long, was funded by the CIA in 2004. And so now we're 20 years into them, starting figuring out these problems, solving these problems, and they've only had an enterprise or corporate product for two years.

So like all these forces are converging, I would love to be able to go back and buy it like in the low-70s. And we may well get a roundtrip back there. But I've never seen a turning point in my career in the way that we're seeing things right now.

Larson: Fair enough. Mike, I want to ask you about the use cases. I kind of think that was one of the most fascinating parts of the story there, that back in December when you talked about Palantir is the different industries that are using their tech, and how is that going to evolve?

Lee: So Palantir's – their CTO goes on CNBC and they - talks about this on their YouTube channel in that, like if you're buying Palantir software, you're looking for ROI day one, not a project that works two and three years down the road where you have like, you buy Salesforce (CRM) and you got to spend more on consultants implementing it than you do on the software. And they're still, look, how well Salesforce has done. Palantir, they want to come in and work on things right away.

So one of the best examples and way to conceptually visualize this is what they've done in hospitals. So if you walk into an ER, there's probably a nurse or a doctor or an administrator, but a couple of people sitting at the front desk taking your data input. And then they're manually sitting there saying, hey, this person has a broken arm, we're going to send him to this room and assign this doctor. That person needs stitches, they'll go over there. This person is having a heart attack, they'll go over there. Well, what happens with – but it's a manual process.

And to try to solve these problems, like you have smart people that have their logic about how they want to allocate doctors to rooms and staff. But their goal for patient care is to get people seen as quickly as possible from a business standpoint, for the hospitals to free up as much emergency room space as possible and get that turnover almost akin to a restaurant.

So at the end of 2022, they had zero hospitals as clients. Now they – now it's close to a quarter of the hospital beds in the country are being run by Palantir. And so what they have done is they've automated that process of allocating patients to rooms, assigning doctors to the rooms, and using machine learning to predict staffing for the hospitals.

And so at Tampa General, right by us, they've been able to do that with 95% accuracy to use machine learning to say, hey, we need this many nurses on staff, this many doctors on staff, okay?

Another unbelievable use case is AIG, just talked about it on their earnings call. In that, Palantir is essential to their underwriting process. And so when they go into underwrite for business, they can produce quotes in three hours in a process that used to take two weeks. And so if you think about what happens there at an underwriting process, you're looking at all the different forms of risk, all the pricing to reinsure it, all the past historical claims that they've had, your predictive models to the future. Historically, there's been a team of smart people sitting there, putting all these pieces together manually and then figuring it out. Hey, that just happens instantaneously right now. Those same smart people are working on the inputs, but the machine is in a sense making the sausage in a couple of hours, what everybody could do in a few weeks.

You can now bid on infinitely more business than you could prior. Time will tell whether it's more predictive, but it's a distinct competitive advantage where there is like almost a day one return of investment, profitable investment for someone going in and buying the software. And how it's gone historically in all these tech trends is first you have the hardware, you build up the infrastructure, then you have the software to implement it.

Larson: Got it. As much as I'd love to talk about Palantir for our entire segment, I think, I'd be cheating our listeners, our viewers, if we didn't look at some of the other ideas that you have out there. I mean, as you survey this market, as we're chatting, NASDAQ has been a little choppy so far this year after a great '24. What other kind of names have you heard about in this earnings cycle that you think have a good story too?

Lee: So again, like I just don't think the market has their arms around how big this AI trend is, and you're looking at trillions of dollars of investment. And if you think about data on a whole as a company takes it in, whatever the organization does, whether it's a nonprofit, whether it's a government organization, whether it's a for-profit company, either a private or public, every bit of data is going to get fed into a large language model at some point in the future.

We do not have anywhere near the amount of infrastructure to figure that out. And so if you look at Microsoft (MSFT) in their earnings, the stock got crushed because they missed on data center growth by, I think, 1.1 percentage point. And Google (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), and Microsoft are all saying the same thing in that they are missing because they are capacity-constrained, meaning the demand far outstrips the supply.

And so, Nvidia, for every GPU they sell, they have demand for 15. And so the Street is looking at all of these names in this complex, like what's the ROI? Like how much earnings am I going to see next quarter without realizing again, like we've just gone from having 50 people in a room, assembling parts by hand to an automated assembly line.

So every organization is going to need to have this as a part of their data intake. And if you look at the way data is being created, so in 2025, we're going to generate as much data as we were predicted to in 2030. And so like the Internet of Things and everything, it's all like a hockey stick growth.

So as more data comes in, as it gets cheaper to compute, the more projects are going to get built out. And like, I don't like to use the word AI or Gen AI because I think of Terminator 2 and like the T-1000 running at you where it's really just – it's just taking data. It's taking that underwriting process that was two weeks long and getting it down to three hours, soon enough an hour, soon enough 30 minutes. Like that's what we're thinking or every single decision, anybody makes of significance is somewhat data-based, and it's organizing all that data and the amount of data that's being created.

So at AMD (AMD), again, like they beat sales, beat estimates, raise guidance, wasn't quite as much as the Street wanted. The stock got crushed. It's trading at 15x forward earnings. And in reality, it's probably more like 12x forward earnings because the earnings will beat. So to me, AMD is just a screaming buy. Their Chief Commercial Officer just bought $1 million worth of stock.

So like you could say whatever you want about insider selling. But what an insider reaches into his own pocket to buy the stock, especially with the stock awards all these executives get in the Q, it's really an unbelievable opportunity.

And so like, obviously, Nvidia is 135, it was at 150, roughly six months ago, and their sales have been – the sales and revenue have increased substantially. The numbers are too low going out a year. So you're looking at '24, '25, four times forward earnings for them, where it's historically traded between 35 and 40.

And then you can go down this infrastructure play, Google, Microsoft, Amazon, just – Oracle (ORCL), all of this, the runway is just so much bigger than the Street’s gives credit for. And again, like this is the one-time in my career where I can just say the majority of sell-side analysts and a majority of buy-side investors, they cannot see the forest from the trees with kind of the single greatest opportunity that's out there.

So I just – seeing this in front of me, the biggest companies, I'm absolutely as bullish as can be. This has nothing to do with tariffs. This has nothing to do with inflation. This has nothing to do with interest rates. This is a trend that's happening, whether you like it or not. And the Street is missing the size of it, which is why like a Palantir can get pushed by mostly retail investors from the low-20s to almost 120 inside of a year because it takes a little bit more vision, a little foresight into the future.

Larson: Got it. You touched on some of those macro points. I think we could probably have that for another discussion. There's a lot to cover there, but I do want to briefly just ask you, given where we are, sort of in the economic cycle, political cycle, whatever, anything outside of tech, sector-wise or name-wise that you think looks pretty attractive here, too?

Lee: So going on that insider buying trend, Ally Financial (ALLY), a lot of the executives went in and bought and like there's a lot of noise around used car defaults and Ally Financial is the old GMAC, doing auto loans, trading a 1x book, 3% dividend yield. It doesn't seem super crazy or sexy. But the reality is like you probably got – you're going to have book value increase over the next year, and it could go to 1.3x or 1.4x book within the next year. It's a 30% or 40% in the sleepy stock. That's where I see some upside there.

And so Tesla (TSLA) is a car company, but it's – so – automated autonomous driving. So Tesla thinks they're going to have their – they're going to have driverless taxis in Austin by the summer, which is mind-boggling. That’s one that – that'll be one of the first things they've had early because that was a 2026 thing. And then they have this robotics division on the other end, and the stock’s at 340, 350 down from over 400, it's worth a look at there. And it's a call option on, okay, do you want a robot in your home to do your laundry, wash your floor, do your dishes, and all sorts of cool stuff thinking two or three years from now?

And then the other one is Mobileye (MBLY). So Mobileye is an autonomous driving software. And so, there's other people out there that have the chips, but Mobileye already has a deal with VW to be the autonomous driving partner for Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY). In 2026, it's going to be in like a third of their cars, but autonomous driving is happening.

And the way that I see it playing out is that people with longer commutes that are driving 20 to 50 minutes on the highway or an hour and a half on the highway to and from work, when you get to level three autonomous driving, you don't need to have your hands on the wheel. You don't need to pay attention. But you can be looking at your phone where if you're in a Tesla right now, and you have the autonomous driving, you don't need your hands on the wheel.

But if you pick up your phone, like you get an alert, like it's not, like the level three, like for those long highway miles, so someone like you going from coast-to-coast, when you're on Alligator Alley, you could be working, instead of paying attention to the road and then from there, I think people get more comfortable with it, taking them off the highway and to their home. So the question for them becomes, do GM and Ford and Honda and Hyundai, okay, do they contract out to Waymo? Do they use Tesla software? Or do they use Mobileye?

Mobileye is your only pure play in the area. The stock has just been crushed. They're already in a lot of cars. If you have a newer car, and you're getting close to somebody and all of a sudden, your car starts automatically breaking and beeping, okay, chances are that's a Mobileye software. So they already have the level three autonomous software that works. And it's just a matter of how many more partners beyond VW can get. And this is an essential component for automakers because this autonomous driving feature will be a monthly payment. You're going to pay $30 or $50. And the first time you saw this was BMW tried to charge for heated seats. It didn't go so well, but there's no reoccurring revenue for these OEMs, for these automakers.

So to be able to charge a subscription fee on a monthly basis to the people that buy their cars, I mean, that's a huge upside. Mobileye will not only get paid to get in the cars, they'll get a percentage of that revenue. So like this could be a 10x in the next four or five years. There's a chance Qualcomm (QCOM) who makes the chips, but doesn't have a software solution, takes them out far above where they are right now. It's a relatively cheap stock. And it's $15, $16, $17 somewhere in there. If they get some good announcements from like a Hyundai or a Honda or Stellantis, your stock could double almost overnight. But I think this is kind of a three-year to five-year play that could easily be a 10x with where autonomous driving is going to happen.

So, I'm sure there's other ideas for trades and other things that are out there. But from my investment standpoint, it's like upside/downside. So you can bang this AI drum, and I'm going to continue to do it, but it’s – the world is changing. We don't have the infrastructure anyway. VRT (VRT) is a company that builds the infrastructure for data warehouses. That's another stock to look at. It just – there's just not enough of this out there. I don't think the Street has their arms around it.

I think people are saying, like Satya Nadella, like, how can you spend $70 billion, $80 billion, $100 billion investing in CapEx on AI. And they're not really seeing the forest from the trees and where Satya Nadella – or I'm sorry, Jensen Huang from Nvidia saying, for every Nvidia GPU Microsoft buys, it could turn around and rent it for $5.

And we're just so far from the end of this trend and there's so much more investment and money to be made and it's going to result in them like a much higher productivity in the S&P 500 in American companies where this is being fully embraced, but that's downstream, right?

So AIG's earnings from Palantir, they're probably seeing something right now in two or three years if none of their competitors buy Palantir, you'll see it there. But it's like I could not be more excited if you can't tell for me, which I think also says a little bit more about me than anything else.

Larson: Well, I'll tell you a true story. I rode my first Waymo when I was just in Phoenix recently and pretty interesting experience, 20-minute drive, give or take and not really any flubs, nothing out of the ordinary. So I hear you on that one.

Anyways, Mike, listen, thank you so much for your insights here.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: Krystal Biotech Soars As Q4 Earnings Put Blockbuster Commercialization In Focus
data.description:
I first covered Krystal Biotech Inc. (KRYS) in December, rating the company a strong buy following FDA approval and rapid commercialization of VYJUVEK, a topical gene therapy for Dystrophic Epidermolysis Bullosa (DEB). On Wednesday, Krystal reported Q4 earnings and updated its guidance for 2025. Q4 earnings were mixed, with Q4 GAAP EPS of $1.58/share beating analysts' expectations by $0.39/share, while revenue of $91.1M (+116.4% YoY) slightly missed analysts' forecast. However, the market moving data from the Q4 earnings call was management's outlook for 2025.

On the call, management reiterated its target for achieving over $1B in peak annual sales for VYJUVEK. The financial guidance was limited to non-GAAP R&D expense, coming in between $150M - $175M. The primary factor that seems to have rallied the stock was the commercialization progress in the EU and Japan, which management identified as a key catalyst for 2025.

In total, I was surprised that the Q4 report, which was more or less in-line with my expectations, prompted a rally from $155/share to $185/share as of this writing. One factor that may be important is the high level of short interest in the stock, with >10% of Krystal's float. Another factor may have been that clinical trials Krystal is engaged in are evolving more quickly than the market was discounting, particularly in cystic fibrosis (CF). Regardless of the rally's cause, I remain bullish on Krystal Biotech and reiterate my STRONG BUY opinion. My 12-month price target is $225/share for this little-known, but already profitable gene-therapy focused biotech company.

Krystal Biotech: Q4 2024 Earnings Call

On the Q4 earnings call, CEO Krish Krishnan began by outlining the firm's strong progress in 2024. VYJUVEK's launch continues to deliver rapid growth and is among the "top tier of rare disease launches." This was achieved through VYJUVEK's robust safety and efficacy in meeting the medical unmet need of DEB. In 2024, Krystal demonstrated that it can safely deliver genes to the lung via a neutralizer. This was a critical challenge in CF and earlier stage programs for NSCLC, validating the lung as a second target tissue for the company's platform after the skin.

Net income was $3.12/share in 2024, up $0.40/share from 2023. Q4 2024 was the sixth straight quarter of positive EPS and the fourth straight quarter of sequential earnings growth. In 2025, Krystal Biotech has three primary goals: first, to bolster its growth trajectory by launching VYJUVEK in Europe and Japan, second to translate early signs of efficacy in CF to advance KB407 in the clinic, and third to prepare for the anticipated launch of ophthalmic B-VEC in patients with ocular complications of DEB.

Since launch, VYJUVEK has realized $341M in net revenue within 18 months. Gross margins of 95% were realized for VYJUVEK in Q4. Given the coming launches in the EU and Japan, management reiterated its confidence that VYJUVEK will achieve blockbuster status, with greater than $1B in annual sales. As yet, VYJUVEK has realized approximately 510 reimbursement approvals as of February 2025. This pace is despite some slowing near the end of 2025 due to the holiday season.

Krystal has been using field tactics driving continued growth in reimbursement approvals and patient demand. DEB is a rare, but serious disease of the skin and muscosal tissues caused by one or more mutations of the COL7A1 gene. The disease can cause painful and debilitating blisters and VYJUVEK is the first redosable gene therapy to deliver the correct COL7A1 gene to patients. As the launch has progressed, patients have achieved durable wound closure and are now able to use the drug for maintenance while leading fuller and more active lives. 97% of treatment occurs in the home and patient compliance remains high at around 85%.

Krystal expects a positive opinion from CHMP by the end of this month, leading to a timeline for the European launch of VYJUVEK to commence in Germany around mid-2025. France would launch shortly after, and the company estimates that over 1,000 patients reside in France and Germany alone, nearly double the number of patients treated to date in the US. In Japan, Krystal has begun hiring its field force. Over 500 patients with DEB are estimated to reside in Japan.

Krystal's proven success in treating COL7A1 gene deficient patients in DEB is the focus of its Ophthalmic B-VEC drug candidate to treat ocular complications of DEB. Up to 25% of patients suffering from DEB have lesions in their eyes that can lead to significant vision loss. To best treat this unmet need, Krystal is initiating a Phase 3 study named IOLITE, with enrollment to date of 50 patients suffering from ocular complication of DEB. This is a new catalyst for Krystal with top-line data expected before the end of 2025.

Krystal had also announced a series of updates for its inhaled programs, which management presented as evidence validating the lung as a second target tissue for its gene-based delivery platform. In the first-in-human Phase 1 study (SERPENTINE-1), KB408 displayed evidence of delivering the SERPINA1 gene for expression in adult patients with Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (AATD). In two patients, evidence of successful gene delivery raised the number of epithelial cells positive for AAT from 3% to 35% and 0% to 39% respectively.

Taking a look at Krystal's pipeline, the number of programs targeting the lung demonstrate the significance of this result. Studies of KB407 and KB408 for CF and AATD depend on targeting the lung in CF and AATD respectively. While in oncology, inhaled KB707 is set to target solid tumors. These programs are earlier, setting commercialization efforts further in the future. However, these exciting results in the lung are a crucial proof-of-concept for Krystal.

In 2025, management identified the following pipeline catalysts for Krystal as follows:

  • Top-line data for KB803 from its Phase 3 for ocular DEB complications.
  • An interim readout from KB304 from its Phase 1 for aesthetic indications.
  • Additional molecular data from Cohorts 2 & 3 for the Phase 1 study of KB408 in AATD.
  • First patient dosed with KB301 in a Phase 2 for dynamic wrinkles of the decollete.

It should be noted that some of these catalysts are for Jeune Aesthetics, Krystal's wholly owned subsidiary focusing on skin health. The most advanced asset of Jeune is KB301 in Phase 2.

Thus the most important catalysts for Krystal Biotech in the coming year are the commercialization progress in Europe and Japan, followed by the anticipated launch of ophthalmic B-VEC after the Phase 3 is concluded and finally progress of KB407 in CF and its ramifications for gene-therapy in the lung.

The primary risk for investors in Krystal is that nearly all present sales are derived from VYJUVEK. Thus, any adverse event slowing the commercialization, such as competition or regulatory difficulties could be a bearish catalyst for the stock.

KRYS Stock: Growth Expectations, Valuation and Price Target

Following $291M in 2024 revenues, Krystal is expected to grow sales to $462M in 2025 as the commercialization of VYJUVEK continues. Management remains positive that the drug will reach peak sales of approximately $1B and analysts see this occurring by 2028.

The acceleration in VYJUVEK sales have already allowed Krystal to achieve profitability. Analysts project that should VYJUVEK growth continue on its current path, that Krystal is priced around 26.5x forward earnings of $7.00/share in 2025.

Given the pace of VYJUVEK's roll out, analysts still see 61% EPS growth in 2026, leading to the conclusion that Krystal remains arguably undervalued relative to its very impressive growth trajectory. Over the next 12-months, I expect that the company's commercialization and opportunity in ocular complications of DEB will extend the growth trajectory. Therefore, investors holding for one-year should expect a price target of $225/share, or 20x 2026 earnings as the rapid pace of growth moderates somewhat.

Conclusion

Krystal's execution in the commercialization of VYJUVEK continues to impress. In addition, new catalysts for continued growth, particularly in ocular complications of DEB are fast approaching in 2025. With Phase 3 results expected this year, the ocular market representing ~25% of the total DEB patients is very material. In addition, Krystal's genetic therapy in lung may provide new markets in AATD, NSCLC and CF, a very exciting but somewhat further off growth catalyst. Krystal Biotech is a gem of a company executing expertly on its gene-delivery platform and remains a STRONG BUY with 22% upside to my 12-month $225/share price target.

data.title: The Tariff Risk Isn't In Inflation (Part II)
data.description:
For Part 1 on “Tariff Risk” read: Tariff Impact Not As Bearish As Predicted.

In “Trumpflation” we discussed why the tariff risk was not inflation. To wit:

“Today, globalization and technology give consumers vast choices in the products they buy. While instituting a tariff on a set of products from China may indeed raise the prices of those specific products, consumers have easy choices for substitution. A recent survey by Civic Science showed an excellent example of why tariffs won’t increase prices (always a function of supply and demand).”

Of course, if demand drops for products with tariffs, prices will fall, reducing inflationary pressures. Furthermore, the tariff risk is not a one-sided event. If Trump tariffs Chinese, European, or Canadian products, those countries tend to enact counter-balancing tariffs on U.S. products. Such slows demand for goods and services between all parties, again a deflationary process.

But therein lies the real tariff risk investors should focus on- the corporate profitability risk.

How We Got Here

Corporate profit margins in the U.S. are at historic highs, with S&P 500 companies enjoying levels well above their long-term exponential growth trends.

Post-pandemic demand surges, supply chain disruptions, and massive fiscal and monetary interventions supported those elevated margins. As evidenced by the chart below, the correlation between economic growth rates and corporate profits is high. Note that outliers of the correlation are historically related to events such as the “Financial Crisis” and post-recession economic recoveries.

However, as the economic landscape shifts, several factors threaten to erode these profit margins, which should raise concerns for investors.

Pandemic-era stimulus measures played a critical role in boosting corporate profits. Trillions in fiscal and monetary support created robust demand, while low interest rates reduced borrowing costs. Businesses capitalized on supply chain disruptions, passing increased costs to consumers with little resistance. At the same time, industries like technology and healthcare benefited from market consolidation, strengthening their pricing power. Labor costs also lagged behind inflation during this period, helping businesses maintain wide margins.

However, these conditions are now waning. As economic growth slows, supply chains normalize, and inflation moderates, sustaining high profit margins will become more challenging.

Such is particularly the case when it comes to tariffs.

Tariff Risk On Corporate Profits

Corporations react to cost increases in their business (i.e., wages, benefits, commodities, utilities, etc.), which must be factored into the selling price to maintain profitability. Crucially, corporations can only pass on higher input costs to consumers if demand remains higher than the available supply of those goods or services.

In 2020 and 2021, corporations could pass on most of the inflationary increase to consumers as they were willing to spend the Government’s money. However, as excess savings run out, inflation declines as consumers decrease spending; corporate profits weaken as the ability to pass on higher input costs to customers fades. As shown, as inflation declines, the rate of change in corporate profits also weakens.

We see the same if you use a two-year average of corporate profits minus inflation. Again, when inflation surged in 2020, corporations could pass on the bulk of the cost increases to consumers. Today, as inflation slows due to declining demand, corporations must absorb the inflation to sell products or services.

Another way to view this issue is by comparing the spread between the consumer price index (what consumers pay for goods and services) and the producer price index (what corporations pay). When inflation rises and consumer demand exceeds supply, corporations can pass higher input costs to consumers. However, when inflation declines, corporations must absorb higher input costs due to slower demand to sell products or services.

Here is the crucial point:

“Corporations don’t create inflation. They merely react to changes in demand and adjust pricing and supply to maintain profitability. When the consumer slows down, corporations cut prices to reduce supply.”

While many risks, such as high labor costs, increased borrowing costs, slowing economic growth, and tax policy risks, threaten corporate profitability over the next few years, tariff risk is often overlooked.

“The narrative in markets is that the outlook for the US is great, and the outlook for Europe, UK, and China is not good. For markets, the problem with this narrative is that 41% of revenues in the S&P 500 come from abroad. If we have a recession in Europe and a continued slowdown in China, it will have a significant negative impact on earnings for S&P 500 companies.” – Torsten Slok, Apollo Academy

One of the risks the Trump administration faces by imposing tariffs is the negative impact of tariffs on exports. Tariffs are an additional tax on imported goods, increasing the costs of those products. However, as noted above, tariffs are never in isolation, as the countries we impose tariffs on will likely impose tariffs back on the U.S. This “tit-for-tat” process threatens to raise costs on exports to countries already impacted by the purchasing power differential caused by a strong dollar. The chart below shows net corporate profit margins during the previous Trump-era tariff policy. Logically, given the high corporate revenue derived from international sales, investors should expect that any cost increase will immediately impact profitability.

With this potential tariff risk in mind, what are the implications for investors in 2025?

Implications for Investors

As always, the problem facing investors is the timing of when the impacts of economic, political, or regulatory changes occur. Sometimes, those impacts can be immediate, such as a reduction in corporate tax rates; other times, the effect of a political or regulatory change can take much longer to manifest.

However, given the exceptionally high profit margin levels in an environment where employment growth is declining, tariff risks are increasing, and economic growth is slowing, being somewhat cautious about specific market exposures may make sense. The chart below shows the long-term relationship between employment growth (where wages and economic demand come from) and corporate profitability as a percentage of the economy.

So, what should investors do? One step would be to monitor portfolio holdings with an exceptionally large foreign revenue exposure. Below is a table of notable S&P 500 companies with substantial international revenue exposure. (Note: The percentages of revenue from international sales are approximate and based on available data as of 2023. Current P/S and P/E ratios are subject to change based on market conditions and company performance.)

Furthermore, investors should adopt a proactive and diversified strategy. The following steps can help mitigate these risks:

  1. Focus on Resilient Sectors: Certain industries, such as utilities, consumer staples, and healthcare, are less sensitive to tariff risks due to consistent demand. Allocating a portion of the portfolio to these sectors can help offset volatility in other areas.
  2. Evaluate Profit Margin Trends: Rising costs, slowing demand, and tariff risks pressure high corporate profit margins. Investors should analyze companies’ ability to maintain profitability amid these challenges. Companies with efficient cost structures and strong pricing power are better positioned to weather such conditions.
  3. Hedge Against Currency Risks: A strong dollar can compound the negative effects of tariffs by making U.S. goods more expensive abroad. Investors can consider hedging strategies or exposure to companies that benefit from a strong dollar.
  4. Adopt a Long-Term View: While tariff policies may create near-term uncertainty, investors should focus on long-term fundamentals. Maintaining a disciplined investment strategy and avoiding reactive decisions based on short-term market volatility can lead to better outcomes.

While I have no idea whether tariff risk will threaten corporate profit margins with certainty, the data suggest that risks exist. As we proceed into 2025, the risk of markets reversing to realign prices with valuations seems increasingly likely.

We suggest that becoming more cautious may pay more significant dividends later this year.

data.title: Crypto ETFs: The Latest Wave Of Filings
data.description:
Some of the most interesting filings in the crypto ETF space are not necessarily new product filings. Potential approval of litecoin (LTC-USD) and XRP (XRP-USD) ETFs have stolen the headlines, in addition to some “creative” filings like dogecoin, Trump coin, and other meme coin ETFs. Recently, however, there have also been several filings to make amendments to existing crypto products. These have flown relatively under the radar. They include potential in-kind redemptions in spot crypto ETFs and potential staking in spot ether ETFs.

In-Kind Redemptions in Crypto ETFs

Recently, Nasdaq filed a proposal with the SEC to allow in-kind Bitcoin redemptions in addition to cash redemptions for the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT). This was followed by a similar filing by the CBOE for the ARK 21Shares Bitcoin ETF (ARKB) and the 21Shares Core Ethereum ETF (CETH). One of the main reasons ETFs are so popular with investors is due to the in-kind creation redemption process. This contributes to their tax efficiency and liquidity. When spot bitcoin ETFs were launched in January 2024, they were approved only for cash redemptions, partly to keep bitcoin out of the hands of authorized participants (AP), due to regulatory concerns. To keep this short, I won’t go into the details of in kind versus cash creation redemption process. For more resources on this process, see these reports by State Street Global Advisors, Charles Schwab Asset Management, and ProShares. Essentially, the cash creation/redemption process adds an extra step of converting between cash and bitcoin with an additional liquidity provider. This way, the AP does not need to “touch” bitcoin.

Grayscale filed a great resource last year - the slide deck discusses some of the benefits of in-kind redemptions, including supporting a deeper, more robust primary market. According to Grayscale, only 15 exchange-traded products are physically-backed commodity funds (not including spot crypto ETFs). All of these use in-kind redemptions. The majority of equity funds also use an in-kind process.

Implications for Investors

It is a common misconception, but in-kind redemptions do not extend to retail investors. So, if you bought and sold shares of IBIT in your brokerage account, you wouldn’t be able to receive any bitcoin in return. Still, this has the potential to create more efficiency and liquidity in the market, as more institutional investors adopt these products. It’s also another step into bringing these crypto ETFs deeper into the mainstream. Last year, IBIT was the third largest ETF by inflows, next to only domestic large-cap heavyweights, the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) and the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV). In-kind redemptions, along with other characteristics, like recent options trading on select spot bitcoin products, contribute to integrating and aligning these products closer to the financial markets and their ETF counterparts.

Staking in Ether ETFs

Staking was another somewhat controversial proposal during the spot ether ETF launch. It is a way of earning extra rewards by holding ethereum. (This is similar to earning interest or dividends, with a few caveats.) As many ether investors know, staking has become a key feature of ether. It was left out of spot ether ETFs because it resembles unregistered securities offerings. On February 12, the CBOE filed a 19b-4 proposal on the behalf of 21Shares to add staking to its 21Shares Core Ethereum ETF (CETF). Soon after, on February 14, the NYSE filed, on behalf of Grayscale, a proposal to add staking to its two ether products — the Grayscale Ethereum Trust ETF (ETHE) and the Grayscale Ethereum Mini Trust ETF (ETH).

Both filings cite the potential use of “point-and-click” staking as a potentially safer method of earning rewards. This process does not involve the staked ether leaving the wallet, which reduces risk of theft.

Implications for Investors

Staking comes with the potential for additional yield for both retail and institutional clients. Allowing staking in these products more closely aligns the investment with direct ether investment. It also improves efficiency and the creation redemption process. Additionally, this would align the U.S. with other countries which have ethereum staking ETPs — for example, the 3iQ Ether Staking ETF (ETHQ:CA) and the 21Shares Ethereum Staking ETP (AETH).

Bottom Line

Keeping track of new crypto product filings has been exciting — especially with the potential for new spot products like litecoin, XRP, solana, and even several more obscure coins. But there is also a maturation of the current crypto framework supporting products belonging to iShares, Grayscale, and more. That maturation could propel the industry forward in terms of efficiency, liquidity, and reputation.

data.title: Unity Software: Tough Transition Still In The Works
data.description:
Unity Software Inc. (NYSE:U) soared on Q4 results, but the software company didn't report the huge turnaround insinuated by the stock gain. The game engine company is poised for a turnaround, but the business still has a long road ahead. My investment thesis remains Neutral on the stock, with 2025 being another transition year.

Mixed Quarter

Unity Software reported a mixed quarter for Q424, with sales plunging 25% while the company made progress with strategic growth initiatives. The company reported the following Q4 numbers:

The strategic portfolio grew 4% YoY to $442 million, with the Grow Solutions finally growing again. The Grow Solutions sales hit $305 million, up from $299 million in the prior quarter, but still down from the prior peak levels.

The Create Solutions were down nearly 50% in the quarter to $152 million. Strategic portfolio sales were up 9% from last Q4 with subscription revenues up 15% YoY, led by 50% growth in the industry revenue.

The numbers are still very messy with the adjustments back in Q423. In essence, Unity hasn't seen much in the way of growth from the new initiatives, with the turnaround still in the works.

Unity Software did announce a promising move to Vector for the shift of the ad tech business to an updated AI version. In addition, the launch of Unity 6 last year and the end of the runtime fees adjusted the business trajectory already.

On the Q424 earnings call, the CEO made the following comments regarding the Unity Vector product:

Vector is designed to leverage data from across the Unity ecosystem, integrating self-learning artificially intelligence models that will provide deeper insights, optimize performance and deliver better results for customers. Vector enhances targeting precision and increases audience scale through a sharper analysis of richer data sets, and it’s also able to adapt in real time, helping customers navigate an increasingly competitive mobile marketing landscape.

Clearly, the market jumped on the possibility from this opportunity due to the huge gains on AppLovin (APP). The ad tech company has seen the stock gain nearly 700% in the last year.

According to Citi analysts, AppLovin only faces a $40 million revenue headwind from the Unity Software ad tech business. AppLovin reported Q4 revenues of $1.4 billion, far above the current levels of Unity.

The game engine company guided to Q125 revenues of only $405 to $415 million. As with a seasonal business, revenues are down substantially from the $457 million just reported in Q4, with only $15 million in quarterly non-strategic portfolio revenues left. In essence, the vast majority of the revenue dip is related to the strategic portfolio.

Unity Software boosted EBITDA margins back to 23%, hitting $106 million in adjusted EBITDA for the quarter. The company beat internal targets of $83 million and topped the $63 million in adjusted EBITDA reported last Q4 in a positive sign of shoring up the financials of the business.

Tough Transition Ahead

The Q1 outlook was a huge disappointment, with consensus revenue estimates up at $441 million. The stock rally seems to be entirely based on the mention of new AI advertising software, though the shift is a big part of the Q1 weakness.

Unity Software will start the integration and customer shift to the Vector product towards the end of Q1. The company won't complete the move until Q2, and the new AI models will take time to ramp up.

AppLovin has absolutely soared in the last few months due to the excitement about advertising and AI. The stock now trades at nearly 30x forward EV/S targets, versus only 7x for Unity Software when updating for the 30% gain.

While Unity has a huge market opportunity, the Vector AI product only transitions at the end of Q1. The company doesn't forecast any material benefits in the near term, as evidenced by the weak guidance for the March quarter.

While the 2025 numbers still include some headwinds from the exited business, analysts only forecast flat sales, prior to the weak guidance. The consensus analyst estimates only forecast 2026 revenue growth of ~9% to reach just $1.94 billion.

Unity Software reported a vastly improved quarter with a turn in the strategic portfolio, but the company still has a long way to go to return to strong growth to warrant a higher stock price beyond the 30% run following Q4 earnings. The company remains a leader in game engine development software. Unity is moving into the XR/AR segments with work on the Android platform and with Metaquest, but those areas are still very small.

Investors should be for a better entry point after weak Q1 numbers and Q2 guidance pulls the stock back down.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Unity Software is already priced for the business reporting solid growth. The company still faces a lot of headwinds and the AI ad tech software will impact revenues as much as help in the 1H of the year.

Investors should look to buy the stock on weakness after the rally to $28.

data.title: Allspring Premier Large Company Growth Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

The fourth quarter of 2024 marked a strong finish to an impressive year for U.S. equities. The broader economy demonstrated resilience, supported by steady inflation, consumer spending, and earnings growth that largely exceeded expectations. Artificial intelligence emerged as a pivotal driver for growth with companies investing heavily to capitalize on its potential. The S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) advanced 2.41% for the quarter and 25.02% for the year. Growth stocks maintained their leadership position for the quarter and the year, as noted by the Russell 1000 Growth Index's 33.36% annual return. While mid- and small-cap stocks experienced periods of favorable leadership during the year, they ultimately lagged their large-cap counterparts.

Driving the quarterly returns was an initial bullish reaction to the U.S. presidential election. Investors responded optimistically to the potential for business-friendly policies and faster economic growth. Following the election, there was an immediate rotation into cyclicals, small-cap stocks, and speculative investments such as cryptocurrencies and lower-quality equities.

The postelection fervor wore off later in the quarter as investors realized that key aspects of the Trump policy agenda could create inflationary pressures. When gross domestic product and inflation came in higher than expected, the U.S. Federal Reserve signaled fewer rate cuts than previously anticipated. A “higher-for-longer” sentiment drove the term premium for the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y) to rise 75 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) during the quarter. As 2024 came to a close, the bullish enthusiasm faltered over the prospects of rising inflation, higher interest rates, and falling stock prices.

The rising interest rate backdrop created a pause in the broadening of market leadership. data.title: The London Company Income Equity Vs. Russell 1000 Value Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market Update

U.S. equities traded higher during 4Q, with most of the major indices posting positive gains. Economic data released during the quarter were positive, but choppy. The Fed's monetary policy continued on a less restrictive path, but shifted a bit more hawkish in December, reflecting higher than desired inflation, a strong labor market and better-than-expected GDP growth in recent quarters. The more hawkish view from the Fed led investors to assume fewer rate cuts in the months ahead. The broader market, measured by the Russell 3000 Index, rose 2.6%. Similar to earlier in the year, larger companies with attractive growth profiles led the market. Looking at market factors, Growth, Volatility, and Momentum posted the strongest returns, while Value, Yield, and most Quality factors presented headwinds.

Key Performance Takeaways

  • The London Company Income Equity portfolio declined 2.2% (-2.3% net) during the quarter vs. a 2.0% decrease in the Russell 1000 Value Index. Stock selection was as headwind to relative performance, partially offset by positive sector exposure.

  • The Income Equity portfolio slightly trailed the Russell 1000 Value in 4Q and fell short of our 75-80% downside capture expectations. Dividend yield headwinds picked up at the end of the quarter, as long-term rates increased. Further, we received limited benefit from our Quality exposure.

  • We continue to believe that Quality factors will add value over full cycles. Our focus on high returns on capital, balance sheet strength, and attractive shareholder yield reinforces our margin of safety and positions our Income Equity portfolio for success in this uncertain climate.

Top 3 Contributors to Relative Performance

  • Apple Inc. (AAPL) - AAPL shares posted solid gains during 4Q after the company released its long-awaited AI product, which kept alive the potential for an iPhone super cycle. R&D remains strong at AAPL, which should lead to solid revenue growth and significant cash flow generation. The company's large installed base is an important long-term driver of the business.

  • Charles Schwab Corporation (SCHW) - Shares of SCHW outperformed as cash sorting issue continues to normalize. Core net new assets also improved/normalized, following the end of the Ameritrade migration. We have conviction that the stock has further room to run over the next 12-18 months as the company's real earnings power and growth potential become evident.

  • BlackRock, Inc. (BLK) - BLK shares outperformed in 4Q reflecting improving organic base fee growth as well as positive market performance. We continue to believe that BLK is positioned well for long-term market share growth through its iShares ETF business and its growing presence in private markets. We also continue to view BLK as a strong fit to our process with its low leverage, growing dividend, and steady repurchase of shares.

Top 3 Detractors from Relative Performance

  • Crown Castle Inc. (CCI) - CCI underperformed during 4Q driven by the lower-than-expected rumored valuation for a potential sale of the fiber/small cell businesses, and slower interest rate cuts in 2025. CCI reported positive tower activity, but canceled some low-return small cell projects, which was viewed as a negative this quarter. The new management team has taken action to improve the return profile of the business and margins have already shown improvement. CCI is in a good position for future growth given its tower locations and U.S.-focused portfolio. We like CCI's stable revenue stream, long-term tailwinds on growth in data consumption, and its ability to return cash to shareholders through its dividend policy.

  • Nestle S.A. (OTCPK:NSRGY) - NSRGY shares underperformed the broader market all year. Sentiment across the packaged food space is low as it emerges from two years of unprecedented food price inflation. NSRGY's latest earnings report did not suggest an acceleration in organic growth from current subdued trends. NSRGY's portfolio is attractively positioned in categories that have stable, long-term volume tailwinds such as coffee, pet food, and nutritional health. Barring further executional missteps, we believe the downside to the current stock price is low.

  • Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) - MRK continues to deliver solid growth in its blockbuster immunotherapy treatment, Keytruda, as well as encouraging contributions from new launches. However, investor sentiment was negatively impacted by a weaker outlook for its HPV vaccine due to challenges in China and broader pharma sector underperformance following the election and health care-related nominations, as the market assesses potential policy implications from the incoming administration. These concerns appear overstated, and we remain confident that MRK's strong portfolio of drugs and vaccines coupled with its effective leadership will create long-term value for shareholders.

Sector Influence

We are bottom-up stock pickers, but sector exposures influenced relative performance as follows:

  • What Helped: Underweight Health Care (a weaker performing sector) & overweight Info. Technology (a better performing sector)

  • What Hurt: Overweight Materials & Consumer Staples (weaker performing sectors)

Trades During the Quarter

  • Increased: Dominion Energy (D) - Addition reflects our growing confidence in the stability of future earnings following negotiations with some of the state Utilities commissions, as well as the sale of some of the non-regulated utilities businesses.

Looking Ahead

As we enter 2025, we believe the market faces an inflection point where sustaining momentum becomes increasingly difficult. Across the real economy, demand still seems sluggish and clear late-cycle signals persist. Revenue growth and corporate profits have leaned on inflationary pricing, but margins face growing headwinds as inflationary pricing fades, input costs rise, and demand softens. The Fed cut rates during 2024, but the yields on longer-dated treasuries actually rose as the year ended. Stubbornly high borrowing costs continue to plague rate-sensitive areas of the economy, like housing. Employment and inflation data may be volatile in 2025 and could affect changes in monetary policy and lead to greater volatility across equity markets.

Despite resilient economic data and limited signs of credit risk, we believe vigilance is warranted. Our cautious posture persists due to high valuations, market concentration, looming debt challenges, and fraying consumer health. We anticipate lower expected returns in the near term, based on slowing growth (function of restrictive monetary policy) and high valuations. Valuation multiple expansion can only take the market so far (particularly late in a market cycle). We expect a reversion to the mean whereby earnings growth & dividends drive returns going forward. While optimism remains high, the vulnerabilities of momentum-driven leadership highlight the need for discipline. Markets may reward risk-taking in the short term, but lasting wealth is built through patience, real income, and fundamentals.

data.title: Allspring Mid Cap Growth Fund Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

The fourth quarter of 2024 marked a strong finish to an impressive year for U.S. equities. The broader economy demonstrated resilience, supported by steady inflation, consumer spending, and earnings growth that largely exceeded expectations. Artificial intelligence emerged as a pivotal driver for growth with companies investing heavily to capitalize on its potential. The S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) advanced 2.41% for the quarter and 25.02% for the year. Growth stocks maintained their leadership position for the quarter and the year, as noted by the Russell 1000 Growth Index's 33.36% annual return. While mid- and small-cap stocks experienced periods of favorable leadership during the year, they ultimately lagged their large-cap counterparts.

Driving the quarterly returns was an initial bullish reaction to the U.S. presidential election. Investors responded optimistically to the potential for business-friendly policies and faster economic growth. Following the election, there was an immediate rotation into cyclicals, small-cap stocks, and speculative investments such as cryptocurrencies and lower-quality equities.

The postelection fervor wore off later in the quarter as investors realized that key aspects of the Trump policy agenda could create inflationary pressures. When gross domestic product and inflation came in higher than expected, the U.S. Federal Reserve signaled fewer rate cuts than previously anticipated. A “higher-for-longer” sentiment drove the term premium for the 10-year U.S. Treasury (US10Y) yield to rise 75 basis points (bps; 100 bps equal 1.00%) during the quarter. As 2024 came to a close, the bullish enthusiasm faltered over the prospects of rising inflation, higher interest rates, and falling stock prices.

Portfolio Positioning: Outlook

The rising interest rate backdrop created a pause in the broadening of market leadership. While a few speculative corners of markets, such as cryptocurrencies and meme stocks, showed momentum, U.S. equities took a generally defensive turn during the quarter. Mega-cap tech stocks resumed market leadership while small caps and more cyclical and rate-sensitive sectors lagged. Our response to this evolving environment was to remain disciplined.

While policy shifts may trigger short-term rotations, we believe that structural growth scarcity should continue to command premium valuations for high-quality growth companies. We avoid chasing cyclical rallies under the banner of "new regimes" and instead keep our focus on businesses with sustainable earnings growth and strong competitive positions. We believe that security selection—not macroeconomic timing—should drive performance in 2025.

We remain excited about the technological innovation inherent in our portfolio companies. We own businesses with durable competitive advantages at the forefront of driving productivity gains. AI spending should remain a key growth driver, potentially ushering in a super cycle of investment that transcends the IT sector. We expect the next phase of AI should shift from building capacity to practical applications in 2025. Our investment process is designed to capture the strong growth potential of these trends.
